synthesis: Variant B wins because it nails the two highest-weighted criteria simultaneously — the hook is viscerally specific and relatable ('I stare at my Meta dashboard every morning'), and the brand voice is authentically human and vulnerable in a way that builds immediate trust with a marketer or founder audience. The confession-to-solution arc is a proven storytelling structure that works across platforms. Its main weakness, shared by all three variants, is the absence of an explicit CTA; borrowing Variant C's directional language ('I'm going to show you every single step') as a follow-on sentence would significantly strengthen B's conversion potential without sacrificing its voice.

scores.reasoning: Personification of the ad account is clever and creates intrigue, but 'something that actually listens' is vague. It stops the scroll with a relatable tension but lacks specificity about what was built.
Conversational, slightly sardonic tone. Feels human and founder-coded. 'Finally built something' has authentic frustration-to-solution energy without sounding corporate.
No explicit CTA. The hook implies a reveal but doesn't direct the viewer to do anything — watch, click, follow, or engage. The payoff is unclear.
Works for Twitter/X or LinkedIn as an opener. Short enough for mobile. But without knowing the platform target, the lack of visual or format cues limits optimization score.

Extremely relatable and specific — 'I stare at my Meta dashboard every morning' is a vivid, concrete scene millions of marketers live in. The vulnerability is disarming and the pivot to 'built agents to read it for me' is a strong payoff. Scroll-stopping.
Authentically human. 'I have no idea what half of it actually means' is the kind of honest admission that builds trust. Zero AI-sounding language. Sounds like a real founder talking to peers.
Implies a solution was built but doesn't explicitly ask the viewer to do anything. Better than A in that 'agents to read it for me' teases a tangible concept, but still no direct CTA.
Perfect length and energy for LinkedIn or Twitter/X. The confessional format performs well on both platforms. Could serve as a video hook or text post opener effectively.

'This is the actual back-end' has some specificity and credibility signals, but opens with 'This is' which is a weak construction. The hook is more of a table of contents than a scroll-stopper. It informs rather than intrigues.

Functional and direct, but slightly mechanical. 'Multi-agent system' and 'analyze a real D2C ad account' sound closer to a product description than authentic founder voice. Not corporate, but not warm either.
'I'm going to show you every single step' is the clearest implicit CTA of the three — it sets an expectation of a tutorial or walkthrough and gives the viewer a reason to keep watching or reading.
Well-suited for YouTube, LinkedIn video, or a thread opener where educational content is expected. 'Back-end' and 'every single step' signal long-form, which fits those contexts well.
